data.title: Crypto (IBIT) Vs. Gold (IAUM): Which Is The Better Hedge?
data.description:
Commodities ETFs To Hedge Against Volatility

When storm clouds began to gather over Wall Street earlier this year, I advised bearish readers to embrace defensive stocks with solid growth, safety, and consistency in the face of a potential downturn. Now that the latest U.S. tariffs against Canada, Mexico and China have wiped $3.4T in “Trump Trades” from the market, even the most steadfast of bulls are looking for ways to protect their portfolios from volatility.

In times of turbulence, I like to utilize a barbell approach of combining fundamentally strong stocks with dividend stocks to provide a steady income stream and mitigate risk. But why stop there? Investors seeking safe havens in the current market downturn might also consider commodities exchange-traded funds (ETFs) as a way to safeguard against volatility.

ETFs exploded in popularity in 2024, with record-breaking growth in assets under management and inflows and over 1,700 new fund launches. Thus far in 2025, many commodities ETFs, which invest in physical assets like gold, have outperformed the market as investors have sought to hedge against inflation, tariffs, trade wars, and natural disasters. Commodities ETFs come with a number of perks:

  • They tend to rise alongside inflation, providing a safety net when purchasing power erodes.

  • They also offer diversification and stability in fluctuating markets, as commodity prices typically move independently of stocks and bonds.

  • Commodity ETFs are also considered relatively safe investments, as long as they offer exposure across various holdings.

  • They also serve as a lower-cost, more accessible route to investing in commodities over trading futures contracts directly.

Natural gas ETFs have notched the most gains so far in 2025, according to ETF database VettaFi, led by the ProShares Ultra Bloomberg Natural Gas (BOIL) ETF’s impressive 61.38% year-to-date return. But natural gas ETFs can be volatile. Their underlying commodity is heavily impacted by deeply fluctuating prices, making them a better fit for short-term bets. For example, the ProShares UltraShort Bloomberg Natural Gas (KOLD) ETF is currently down 52.6% YTD.

Gold ETFs are similarly outpacing the market, with an average three-month return of 8.42% across 18 different funds, according to VettaFi. That’s compared to the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trusts’ (SPY) 2.15% YTD return, which tracks the broader market. Gold is often considered a better hedge against volatility because it tends to increase in value when the dollar loses its strength. For investors in search of downside protection and solid returns, gold may be the right category for you. Corn (11.99% average YTD return), Copper (11.41% average YTD return), Broad Agriculture (7.59% average YTD Return), and Broad Precious Metals (6.32% average YTD return) are also among the top performers in today’s market, per VettaFi’s list. But as digital currencies take the world by storm, and the positive correlation between Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and gold amid volatility takes shape, cryptocurrencies have been viewed as commodities given their decentralized nature and use as a medium of exchange.

Is Crypto A Commodity?

While commodities have traditionally been defined as raw physical goods, that definition has been changing since the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) stepped in and began regulating Bitcoin (BTC-USD) in 2015. “Depending on its design, function, and use, a digital asset may be characterized differently, including as a commodity, swap or other derivative,” the CFTC has clarified. Of course, this commodity category doesn’t quite fit with the others in terms of being a safe haven investment.

While cryptocurrency may be a riskier investment, digital assets can still have inflation and volatility-hedging characteristics. Bitcoin, for example, has a set amount of 21M coins globally, making it resistant to inflation. Its scarcity and fixed supply have even earned it the nickname “digital gold.”

So if gold ETFs are among the highest-returning, anti-inflationary commodities in the market currently, could “digital gold” be just as good?

Hot Commodities: Gold And Bitcoin

Bitcoin surged to all-time highs in 2024 but turned lower in early 2025 as market volatility picked up. At the same time, gold moved in the opposite direction, hitting record highs. The precious metal benefited from rising U.S. inflation expectations, a weaker U.S. dollar, and increasing bets on Federal Reserve rate cuts.

The chart below compares how gold and Bitcoin move relative to the S&P 500 each month (by percent change). Both have a positive correlation with stocks, but Bitcoin is far more sensitive—about 19 times more than gold!

Another way to see this is by comparing their 20-day moving average correlations with the S&P 500. On average, Bitcoin’s correlation with stocks has been 0.2 points higher than gold’s. While this means Bitcoin tends to fall harder during volatility, it also has the potential to outperform gold when stocks recover.

Since 2022, Bitcoin’s average correlation with the S&P has been +0.2 points higher than gold’s correlation, further suggesting that Bitcoin is more sensitive to market downturns. To investigate further, let’s examine a top Quant-ranked gold ETF and a top-ranked Bitcoin ETF.

How We Measure Top ETFs

According to SA’s Quant system, the iShares® Gold Trust Micro ETF (NYSEARCA:IAUM) is currently the second best-performing fund among all commodities ETFs, with a Strong Buy Quant rating and excellent ETF Factor Grades. When it comes to crypto, the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (NASDAQ:IBIT) is among the top Quant-ranked alternative ETFs and digital asset ETFs. Our ETF grades differ slightly from Quant Factor Grades, as ETFs are technically investment funds and therefore need to be evaluated for portfolio-level characteristics like tracking error and liquidity, among others. However, SA Quant ETF grades leverage the same rigorous, data-driven approach to provide an instant characterization of critical ETF attributes. SA Quant evaluates ETFs for the following attributes:

  1. Momentum, which measures the ETF’s price performance against its peers. Strong historical performance can be a highly predictive factor of a fund’s future returns.

  2. Expenses, which evaluate how an ETF’s expense ratio ranks compared to similar funds, are crucial as higher fees ultimately reduce investor returns. This also takes into account a fund’s bid-ask spread, which is the difference between the buying and selling price of assets. A narrower bid-ask spread is a sign of market efficiency and saves investors money.

  3. Dividend grades, which assess the growth and consistency of the ETF’s dividends alongside its yield profile, are crucial for income-seeking investors.

  4. Risk, where a grade is derived based on multiple risk factors, including standard deviation, tracking error, and turnover, helps investors understand the fund's potential volatility and downside.

  5. Liquidity characteristics, like trading volume and assets under management, impact how easily investors can buy or sell shares at good prices.

IBIT vs. IAUM: Which Is The Better Hedge?

When compared side-by-side with the S&P, the iShares® Gold Trust Micro ETF easily outperforms both the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF and the broader market on a one-year basis.

However, on a six-month basis–particularly as volatility has picked up since November 2024–IBIT has risen to eclipse the gold ETF and the broader market.

Let's examine the quantitative figures more closely to determine which is the better hedge.

iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF

  • AUM: $51.6B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Asset Class: Alternative ETFs

  • Sub Class: Digital Assets ETFs

  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 3/7/2025): 3 out of 292

  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 3/7/2025): 3 out of 46

  • Expense Ratio: 0.12%

IBIT is the #3 Quant-ranked Alternative ETF and Digital Asset ETF launched by BlackRock in January 2024. With more than $50B in AUM, IBIT is BlackRock’s flagship spot Bitcoin ETF. The fund holds Bitcoin in a trust structure, providing investors with a regulated and convenient way to gain exposure to the cryptocurrency. IBIT boasts an ‘A’ expense grade, with a bid-ask spread of 0.02% and an expense ratio of just 0.12%, making the ETF an attractive alternative to investors who would otherwise pay hefty recurring fees on crypto wallets or exchanges.

IBIT ETF Grades

With an ‘A+’ Momentum Score, IBIT has returned around 33% over the past year and nearly 67% over the past six months. The strong momentum comes amid increased investor interest in crypto, particularly since the election victory of Donald Trump, who has pledged to make America “the crypto capital of the planet.” That has contributed to the ETF’s exceptional liquidity and relatively low price tag at $50.64 per share. Cheap fees make IBIT a solid choice for investors curious about crypto. However, the ETF has shaved off around 4.2% YTD amid the current market downturn, begging the question of whether it’s a solid option for hedging against volatility or whether it’s better off as a more experimental segment within a well-balanced portfolio.

iShares® Gold Trust Micro ETF

  • AUM: $1.8B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Asset Class: Commodities

  • Sub Class: Commodities Focused

  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 3/7/2025): 2 out of 61

  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 3/7/2025): 2 out of 41

  • Expense Ratio: 0.09%

IAUM ranks the highest among eight gold ETFs with Strong Buy Quant Ratings in the Commodities Asset Class, second in the category only to the United States Natural Gas Fund, LP ETF (UNG). Backed by physical gold bars held in vaults, IAUM offers investors a more accessible entry point to investing in gold without needing to own the physical commodity. The ETF is the lowest-cost physical gold Exchange Traded Product (ETP) on the market, making it an affordable option for investors looking for ways to hedge against sticky inflation. With straight A’s in every category, IAUM provides steady returns at an exceptional price.

IAUM ETF Grades

Momentum: IBIT vs. IAUM ETFs

IBIT has struggled to perform in recent months, but strong six and nine-month performances have beaten the broader market. IAUM has seen strong price momentum as investors have clung to gold amid lingering inflation. At a six-month total return of 16.2%, a 3.3% difference from the median, the gold ETF has significantly outperformed the wider ETF universe under mounting volatility.

Liquidity: IBIT vs. IAUM ETFs

IBIT is highly liquid, with a three-month average daily dollar volume representing the total value of shares traded on average over the past three months of 2.64B. That dwarfs the 1.73M median for all ETFs by more than 152,297%. IAUM is also highly liquid, with an average 3M daily dollar volume of 52.10M, 2,910.79% from the 1.73M median, at an AUM of $51.56B.

While both ETFs have strong liquidity, one carries significantly more risk.

Risk: IBIT vs. IAUM ETFs

IBIT’s ‘F’ risk score is underlined by an ‘F’-rated standard deviation of 56.46, a 346% difference from the 12.7 ETF median, and similarly poor annualized volatility of 54.8%. IAUM’s ‘A’ risk score indicates a far safer bet, with a ‘B-’ rated standard deviation of 11.88 (a -6.2% difference to the RTF median) and ‘C’-rated annualized volatility of 15.23% (a 14.9% difference from the 13.3% ETF median).

Expenses: IBIT vs. IAUM ETFs

IBIT has an enticing 0.12% expense ratio and 0.02% bid/ask % spread, a -76% and nearly 88% difference from their respective ETF medians.

IBIT Expense Grade

IAUM also scores A’s in both expense categories at similarly attractive distances from the medians.

IAUM Expense Grade

With both ETFs touting strong expense, liquidity, and momentum grades, the key difference comes down to risk.

Concluding Summary: All That Glitters Isn’t Gold

ETFs continued their remarkable growth trajectory in 2024, with global assets surpassing $15T and U.S. inflows exceeding $1T. Investors looking to hedge against volatility in the current market face no shortage of compelling options. Still, a Strong Buy-rated gold ETF such as IAUM is a slightly safer bet than an equally Strong Buy-rated Bitcoin ETF such as IBIT. Gold is less correlated with the U.S. stock market and has thus far better demonstrated its ability to insulate one’s portfolio against inflation while providing healthy diversification in the current market. However, investors with hearty risk appetites might still consider IBIT as a lucrative gateway into the crypto world for its excellent momentum, attractive price, and fantastic liquidity.

We have many ETFs with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's ETF Ratings Screener tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.
